frontmatter.title: Glossary
frontmatter.description: Glossary of terms used in Pythia Foundations.

repository, providing a seamless way to share and interact with code and data.

. The Binder links on most Project Pythia pages point to

. BinderHub deploys and manages the interactive computing environments for

A separate workspace to make and track changes without impacting other branches of the code
An error or flaw in software.
command used to create a local copy of a remote repository.
Text-based programs that users interact with via the terminal application or command line.
A snapshot of your Git repository at a specific time.

Conda is an open-source, cross-platform, language-agnostic package manager and environment management system that allows you to quickly install, run, and update packages within your work environment(s). To install conda, we recommend
in the context of Project Pythia.
conda-forge is a large, community-organized collection of software packages that can be installed on a wide variety of computing platforms via the
package manager. It is our recommended installation source for Python itself as well as most packages in the
The integration and automation of practices and processes for software development and operations.
repositories that allows for things like sharing project related announcements, gauging opinions through polls, hosting planning discussions, and Q&A.
Software documentation is descriptive content (e.g., text, images, videos) that is either embedded in the software code or distributed alongside the corresponding software.
A collaborative development workflow where new development takes place on dedicated branches rather than the main branch.
A copy of another project hosted on a collaborative development platform such as
A collaborative development workflow where new development takes place on forked repositories rather than the main project repository.
Software that is free of charge and where the underlying code is publicly available.
A web platform for collaborative software development.
A common feature of collaborative development platforms such as
used to track bugs, request features, and manage work related for a specific repository.

files) are computational narrative documents that can contain executable code, LaTeX equations, visualizations (e.g., plots, pictures), and richly formatted text. The code does not have to just be Python, other languages such as Julia or R are supported as well. Jupyter Notebooks are celebrated for their interactive output that allows movement between code, code output, explanations, and more code — similar to how scientists think and solve problems. Jupyter Notebooks can be thought of as a living, runnable publication and make for a great presentation platform. See also
Software engines and their environments (e.g., conda environments) that execute the code contained in a
A popular web application on which users can create and execute
files, as well as explore data, install software, etc. You can find more information on running JupyterLab
A web-based platform that authenticates users and launches
applications for users on remote systems.
A free and open-souce operating system based on
Refering to something, such as a
repository, on your computer rather than a remote server.
A simple, human-readable text language used extensively in

and other code repositories, and elsewhere on the Internet (e.g., forums and blogs). Markdown lets us write readable plain text that can rendered as nicely styled text on web pages and other media, with headings, links, and other formatting.
A specific type of commit that combines changes from two branches.

A Python package is a collection of modules, which, in turn, are essentially Python scripts that contain published functionality. There are Python packages for data input, data analysis, data visualization, etc. Each package offers a unique toolset and may have its own unique syntax rules. You can install Python packages with
A storage space containing all of the files and revision history for a software project. Also known as a repo.
A repository hosted on a server such as
s that support analysis, manipulation, and visualization of scientific data.
The project repository from which a fork was created.
A software practice for tracking and managing changes to code and other information stored in
Software tools that track and manage changes to project code and files over time.
